title: Corrections to “A Signature-Constrained Two-Stage Framework for Hyperspectral Target Detection Based on Generative Self-Supervised Learning”
content: The signature-constrained two-stage framework for hyperspectral target detection (HTD) (IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing (IEEE TGRS), 5514917, 2025) needs to predict whether the label of each encoded feature vector belongs to the target category. The process computes the inner product between the feature vector and each signature feature vector. It claims that the product reflects the similarity between the two vectors, with higher values indicating greater similarity. We find this claim is generally not valid without further assumptions. We stress that the general inner product cannot be used to indicate the Euclidean distance between two vectors. We show that the distance-compatible similarity score can reflect the true similarity between any two vectors with the same dimension.
author: Zhengjun Cao, Olivier Markowitch

title: Change-LISA: Language-Guided Reasoning for Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) is widely used for monitoring land-surface dynamics. Most models, however, output an unconditional ”all-changes” map and do not account for user intents expressed in natural language. This can lead to spurious masks under counterfactual (i.e., negative/mismatched) queries. This article studies language-guided reasoning change detection. Given a bitemporal image pair and a text instruction, the model outputs an instruction-conditioned change mask; the correct output can be an empty mask when the queried change is absent. To support this setting, ReasonRS-110K is built as a benchmark of 112 317 image–instruction–mask triplets. Instructions are organized into hierarchical reasoning levels (L1–L3) together with L0 negative queries for compliance evaluation. Change-LISA is proposed as a parameter-efficient framework that combines a frozen Siamese SAM encoder and an explicit difference/fusion module with a multimodal LLM (LLaVA). The LLM emits a token whose embedding prompts a lightweight mask decoder. Only low-rank adaptation (LoRA) adapters and the decoder are optimized. On ReasonRS-110K, Change-LISA reaches 63.1 IoU and  $77.3~{F}1$  on positive-query levels (L1–L3) and 95.4 NCA with 0.12 FPA on L0 negative queries, outperforming representative change detection (CD)-only, referring image segmentation (RIS), and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) baselines. The code for this work is available at https://github.com/jxy0413/changeLisa
author: Xiangyu Jia, Zhibo Chen, Shengyi Zhang, Xiaojing Xue

title: NASA Multifrequency Radar for Hydrometeor Phase Identification
content: This study investigates the potential of dual-frequency/multifrequency Doppler radar for hydrometeor phase classification and develops a phase identification algorithm using NASA airborne radar observations. Theoretical model simulations confirm a clear distinction in both Doppler velocities (V) and dual-frequency Doppler velocities (DDVs)—the difference in V at two radar frequencies—between snow and rain hydrometeors. This distinction provides a useful feature for phase identification. A statistical analysis of V and DDV was performed using airborne Doppler measurements from NASA’s multifrequency (X-, Ku-, Ka-, and W-bands) radar collected during the investigation of microphysics and precipitation for atlantic coast—threatening snowstorm (IMPACTS) field campaign. Results show that snow can be separated from rain using either V or DDV. However, the linear depolarization ratio (LDR) data are required to differentiate snow/rain from mixed-phase (or melting) hydrometeors, as Doppler data alone are insufficient. While the differences in V between snow and rain were found to be larger than in DDV, the air motion independence of DDV is a key advantage. Therefore, a technique leveraging both V and DDV is developed. By combining V, DDV, and LDR, this algorithm successfully classifies hydrometeor phases using the NASA airborne radar dataset.
author: Liang Liao, Robert Meneghini, Gerald M. Heymsfield, Hyokyung Kim, Mei Han

title: S3NN: Exploring Stochastic Modeling in Precipitation Nowcasting Domain
content: Precipitation nowcasting based on artificial intelligence has gained significant attention in both meteorology and computer science communities in the last few years. While researchers continuously introduce new models to improve forecasting performance, it is worth noting that most of these models are deterministic. Unfortunately, it is difficult for deterministic models to model future uncertainty, visually shown as blurry. Stochastic generative models offer a feasible solution; they are dedicated to learning the data distribution and generating new samples from it. Differently, ensemble models deal with uncertainty through probability processing. Inspired by ensemble models, we view precipitation nowcasting as a stochastic process. We propose a new model named stochastic neural network (SNN), which samples high-quality frames from a collection of proposal frames to compose the final prediction. The ongoing debate about which model is better has not yet concluded, and the better approach is to integrate and absorb their respective strengths. By combining SNN with two existing stochastic generative models, the generative adversarial network (GAN) and the variational auto-encoder (VAE), we propose S3NN to model uncertainty and mitigate ambiguity in precipitation nowcasting. S3NN can operate in member-mode and ensemble-mode simultaneously, requiring only one end-to-end training. This enables it not only to produce accurate precipitation forecasts but also to perform probabilistic forecasts, the latter being extremely useful in estimating probabilities of excessive precipitation. Finally, we perform extensive experiments on two radar datasets named HKO-7 and MeteoNet, and the results validate the superiority of S3NN.
author: Zhifeng Ma, Weifang Zhang, Yuanfeng Li, Yile Li, Lei Feng, Lingyun Feng, Bing Xu, Hao Zhang, Jie Liu

title: A Physics-Based Feature XGBoost Model for Frozen Ground Surface Reflectance Reconstruction on the Tibetan Plateau
content: Surface reflectance (SR) variability in frozen ground regions is critical for understanding land surface processes and their response to climate change. However, traditional physical models rely on complex input parameters, limiting their adaptability across diverse environments, while statistical and machine learning approaches often neglect the underlying physical mechanisms, resulting in constrained reconstruction accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose a physics-based feature XGBoost (PF-XGBoost) model for SR reconstruction in frozen ground regions. The model incorporates two physically meaningful components—volume scattering and surface scattering—as prior features to characterize SR mechanisms. These physical priors are further integrated with normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) and land surface temperature (LST), which describe vegetation conditions and thermal states, thereby forming physics-based features and a data-driven reflectance reconstruction framework. The model was trained and evaluated using moderate resolution imaging spectroradiometer (MODIS) data across different seasons and frozen ground regions on the Tibetan Plateau (TP) and validated against in situ measurements and global land surface satellite (GLASS) albedo products. Experimental results demonstrate that PF-XGBoost achieves high reconstruction accuracy across regions and seasons ( $\mathbf {R^{2} \gt 0.85}$ ,  $\mathbf {RMSE \lt 0.025}$ , and  $\mathbf {MAPE \lt 10{\% }}$ ), and shows strong agreement with both in situ and GLASS data ( $\mathbf {R^{2} \gt 0.85}$ ). Furthermore, the model-reconstructed seasonal decline in reflectance during summer corresponds well with observed increases in surface temperature over the QTP. Compared to conventional physical models, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and random forests (RFs), PF-XGBoost demonstrated an R2 improvement of approximately 0.027. Further analysis suggested that incorporating physical priors was essential to enhancing Fmodel stability and accuracy. Overall, the proposed method provides a high-precision, physically consistent solution for frozen ground reflectance reconstruction, with strong regional adaptability and promising application potential.
author: Bo Wang, Kerui Li, Qinghong Sheng, Jun Li, Yuejie Zhang, Xiao Ling, Xiang Liu, Yichen Guo, Yihui Wang, Yuxin Zhu

title: Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation Based on Boundary Awareness and Multiscale Spatial Descriptors
content: The 3-D point cloud semantic segmentation is essential for 3-D environmental perception and scene understanding. A key challenge lies in enhancing object boundary segmentation accuracy and capturing global contextual features efficiently. However, most existing methods optimize boundaries only through local feature enhancement or explicit boundary optimization, and struggle to comprehensively capture global information. Therefore, we propose BAMS-Net, a novel point cloud semantic segmentation network, integrating boundary awareness and multiscale spatial descriptors (MSDs). Our model innovatively fuses local feature enhancement with explicit boundary optimization in depth, enabling improvement in boundary delineation. Specifically, candidate boundary points are first determined through a boundary prediction module based on local neighborhood semantic consistency, and an adaptive weight learning mechanism is adopted to suppress the feature interference from boundary points in their neighborhoods. Then, the boundary segmentation performance is further improved through a boundary optimization aggregation loss that combines local semantic boundary loss and adaptive aggregation loss. To effectively capture global context, an adaptive calibration module is used to reweight the multilevel local features and strengthen the critical information, and then, the enhanced local features of each layer are converted into a multiscale global descriptor through cross-level fusion, thereby efficiently capturing global contextual information. The experimental results on the Stanford large-scale 3-D indoor spaces (S3DIS) and SensatUrban datasets demonstrate that BAMS-Net extracts richer point cloud features, delivers precise object boundary segmentation, and achieves superior overall performance in point cloud semantic segmentation.
author: Haoxue Jia, Xiaoli Liang, Yunxiang Liu, Zhiqiang Qin, Ke Liu, Cancong Dai

title: A Probabilistic Optimization Framework for Terrestrial and UAV-Borne Laser Scanning Forest Point Cloud Registration
content: Registering 3-D point clouds from terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) and uncrewed aerial vehicle-borne laser scanning (ULS) is essential for comprehensive forest-structure analysis. However, large cross-platform disparities in sampling density, occlusion, and viewpoint often degrade existing registration methods in complex stands. We present a probabilistic registration framework tailored to TLS–ULS data fusion. First, a sparse set of topologically salient keypoints is extracted using a discrete Morse-theoretic pipeline, yielding substantial data reduction while retaining structural signal. We then formulate alignment as a maximum-likelihood problem that balances geometric similarity with spatial proximity, solved via an expectation–maximization (EM) scheme with deterministic annealing, followed by a trimming keypoint-based fine-alignment stage. We evaluate the proposed method on eight real-world forest plots against four strong baselines, including two recent optimization-based approaches and two representative learning-based models. Our method consistently delivers improved accuracy and robustness, achieving a mean translation error of 4.0 cm while using less than 5% of the input points. Overall, the proposed framework offers a practical and efficient solution for TLS–ULS registration, enabling reliable downstream quantification of forest structure.
author: Yiliu Tan, Xin Xu, Jingyi Zhang, Tianyu Wu, Di Wang, Wushuang Wang, Xin Yang, Quang Nguyen Huu, Maiko Shigeno

title: TAPTrack: An Efficient Temporal-Aware Prompt Tracker for Infrared Anti-UAV Tracking
content: With the widespread application of uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), efficient supervision and tracking have become urgent challenges. Tracking UAVs in infrared imaging is particularly difficult due to small size, sparse features, and significant susceptibility to camera motion and background clutter. Mainstream high-precision anti-UAV trackers adopt a global tracking paradigm; however, their inference speed often fails to meet the real-time demands of practical deployment. In contrast, the local tracking paradigm based on Transformer offers superior real-time performance. We found that enhancing the search factor (SF) can improve performance to some extent; however, this enhancement also introduces increased background noise. To address these issues, we propose an efficient local tracker, temporal-aware prompt tracker (TAPTrack), specifically designed for infrared anti-UAV tracking. This method expands the SF while utilizing tracking results from previous frames as temporal prompts to guide the extraction of features from the search area. In addition, a temporal prompt decoder (TPD) is developed to mitigate interference resulting from extensive searches. Furthermore, a training-friendly state discrimination mechanism is introduced, which combines the outputs of dual tracking heads with the peak-to-sidelobe ratio (PSR) to determine the target’s status, thereby streamlining the training process. Finally, systematic evaluations were conducted on three infrared anti-UAV tracking benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrate that TAPTrack achieves superior performance among local trackers, showing a 2.1% improvement over the second-best method in the SA metric, while maintaining a real-time inference speed of 182 FPS. Our code will be released on https://github.com/cui344119693/TAPTrack
author: Xiaolong Cui, Xingxiu Li, Panlong Wu, Xinan Liu, Shan He

title: A Two-Stage Surface Energy Balance-Guided Graph Neural Network for Reconstruction of Missing Land Surface Temperature
content: Land surface temperature (LST) is a key parameter in the land surface energy cycle. However, due to cloud contamination, LST products derived from thermal infrared (TIR) remote sensing exhibit significant missing areas. Although many studies have attempted to reconstruct missing areas using reanalysis data, such as the land component of the fifth generation of European ReAnalysis (ERA5 Land), existing methods neglect the temporal differences between MODIS and reanalysis data, suffering from several limitations in applicability. Therefore, a two-stage surface energy balance-guided graph neural network (TSSEB-GNN) was developed, with the innovation of coupling the network with physical mechanisms. First, the physical factors influencing LST were determined based on surface energy balance (SEB) theory and the Bowen ratio. Second, the stage one model based on a graph neural network (GNN) module was designed to generate an initial gap-free LST. Third, the second-stage model fuses the initial LST by adding sparse and dense self-attention (SDSA) mechanisms and incorporating 12 physical factors to capture spatial and temporal differences. The results show that the TSSEB-GNN model achieves a root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 2.56–3.45 K under clear-sky conditions and 3.39–3.63 K under cloudy-sky conditions. Compared with existing methods, TSSEB-GNN not only provides higher accuracy but is also less affected by long-term gaps in MODIS LST. Ablation experiments demonstrate that the TSSEB-GNN model achieves the best performance by accounting for both temporal and spatial differences. The results highlight the effectiveness of the TSSEB-GNN, its interpretability, improved applicability, and greater potential for large-scale applications.
author: Zhenwei Wang, Yingbao Yang, Xin Pan, Penghua Hu, Yuncheng Chen, Zi Yang, Xiaoxiao Kong, Yang Dai

title: MSDCN-LSTF: A Multiscale and Multidimensional Collaboration Network for Land Surface Temperature Spatiotemporal Fusion Based on Transformer and Convolution
content: High spatiotemporal resolution land surface temperature (LST) data are crucial for dynamic environmental monitoring. Spatiotemporal fusion is an effective means to obtain high spatiotemporal resolution remote sensing data. However, existing spatiotemporal fusion methods are still insufficient in dealing with the uncertain temporal fluctuations and complex spatial distribution of LST data. In this article, we propose a multiscale and multidimensional collaboration network combining a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a Transformer to fuse Landsat and MODIS LST products, which explicitly models spatial scale discrepancies, multidimensional feature interactions, and unstable spatiotemporal variation patterns. Specifically, a multiscale and multidimensional attention module (MSMDAM) is designed to enable collaborative interaction across channel–spatial dimensions and adjacent scale levels, enhancing the representation of complex spatial structures in LST. To mitigate redundant information in self-attention computation, an adaptive sparse transformer (AST) is introduced to jointly model sensor bias and temporal variations. In addition, a novel weighting strategy (STS weighting) that considers the stability of spatiotemporal variation trends is designed to further address the rapid temporal and spatial changes of LST. Experiments conducted over three study areas demonstrate that MSDCN-LSTF generally outperforms existing advanced spatiotemporal fusion methods, achieving an average root-mean-square error (RMSE) below 1.53 K and an average structural similarity (SSIM) exceeding 0.97, with RMSE reductions ranging from 0.39 to 1.40 K. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of MSMDAM and STS-weighting, which reduce RMSE by 0.28 and 0.39 K, respectively. These results indicate that MSDCN-LSTF provides a robust and effective approach for high-precision LST spatiotemporal fusion.
author: Mengmeng Wang, Xinyuan Chen, Guizhou Wang, Renfeng Wang, Lin Du, Zhengjia Zhang

title: 10k Is Enough: An Ultralightweight-Binarized Network for Infrared Small-Target Detection
content: The widespread deployment of infrared small-target detection (IRSTD) algorithms on edge devices necessitates the exploration of model compression techniques. Binarized neural networks (BNNs) are distinguished by their exceptional efficiency in model compression. However, the small size of infrared targets introduces stringent precision requirements for the IRSTD task, while the inherent precision loss during binarization presents a significant challenge. To address this, we propose a binarized IRSTD network (BiisNet), which preserves the core operations of binarized convolutions while integrating full-precision features into the network’s information flow. Specifically, we propose a dot binary convolution (DB Conv) to retain fine-grained semantic information in feature maps while still leveraging the binarized convolution operations. In addition, we introduce a smooth and adaptive dynamic softsign (DySoftSign) function, which provides more comprehensive and progressively finer gradients during backpropagation, enhancing model stability and promoting an optimal weight distribution. The experimental results demonstrate that BiisNet achieves a mean intersection over union ( $mIoU$ ) of 68.23% with only 10k parameters, delivering an 11.56% improvement over the previous best binary neural network while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art (SOTA) full-precision models. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/joeyxin-del/BiisNet
author: Biqiao Xin, Qianchen Mao, Bingshu Wang, Jiangbin Zheng, Yong Zhao, C. L. Philip Chen

title: DBSAM: A Dual-Branch Segment Anything Model for Infrared Small Target Detection
content: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to the inherently low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), complex background clutter, and indistinct target boundaries. To tackle these interconnected issues, we propose a novel dual-branch network based on the segment anything model (DBSAM) that couples background suppression with explicit edge structure modeling. The architecture consists of two specialized components: 1) within the encoder, our proposed adaptive wavelet-based background suppression (AWBS) modules employ a stationary wavelet transform (SWT) with adaptive thresholding to simultaneously denoise encoder features and enhance target saliency through multiscale decomposition and 2) in parallel, the edge-aware fusion branch (EAFB) processes the Sobel-derived edge maps utilizing dynamic snake-like receptive fields to precisely capture fine-grained boundary structures. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that DBSAM outperforms existing methods across multiple evaluation metrics, including detection probability ( $P_{d}$ ), false-alarm rate ( $F_{a}$ ), and intersection over union (IoU). Ablation studies further validate the contributions of both AWBS and EAFB to enhance overall performance.
author: Ziyi Zheng, Weixing Li, Feng Pan, Ronghao Wang, Qi Gao

title: Identification and Observation of Spatiotemporal Evolution Features of Coalfield Fire Areas Using Landsat Time-Series Decomposition
content: Globally uncontrolled and persistent coal fires cause substantial resource depletion and long-term environmental and safety risks. However, their long-term detection is hindered by complex subsurface dynamics, combustion behavior, and insufficient data from traditional monitoring methods. This study developed an integrated framework for the dynamic characterization of fire hazards. The approach combines time-series decomposition, abrupt change analysis, and robust regression, using Landsat-derived land surface temperature (LST) time-series data (2013–2024) for the Miquan coalfield, Xinjiang, China. The LST was first retrieved from Landsat thermal infrared (TIR) imagery via the radiative transfer equation (RTE) model. A hybrid seasonal-trend decomposition using loess (STL) and breaks for additive season and trend (BFAST) model then separated trend, seasonal, and residual components, enabling the detection and quantification of abrupt LST changes related to coal-fire dynamics. Thermal trends and anomalies were further quantified using the Mann–Kendall (MK) test, Sen’s slope, and a novel average annual deviation (AAD) metric. Crucially, using random sample consensus (RANSAC)-based regression, persistent potential fire pixels were extracted and categorized into four frequency regimes, with high robustness to noise and data gaps. The resulting coal-fire frequency maps achieved over 77% spatial accuracy relative to field records, demonstrating enhanced continuity and reliability in long-term coal-fire monitoring. The framework provides a versatile, pixel-level, and analytical paradigm for understanding the spatiotemporal evolution of these challenging landscape phenomena.
author: Jinchang Deng, Bobo Shi, Yong Xue, José L. Torero Cullen, Liying Han, Zhengyang Qu, Jin Li

title: Estimating Maximum Usable Frequency of HF Communication Links Using the Low-Latitude Long-Range Ionospheric Radar
content: High-frequency (HF) radio communication is essential for long-range connectivity in remote and maritime regions lacking conventional communication infrastructure. The maximum usable frequency (MUF), which is critical to HF effectiveness, is sensitive to dynamic ionospheric conditions. Traditional MUF observations and global models suffer from limited coverage or reduced accuracy, particularly over low-latitude oceanic regions where ionospheric variability is pronounced. This study introduces a practical MUF inversion method for low-latitude regions, utilizing ground/sea scatter echoes from the low-latitude long-range ionospheric radar (LARID) at Dongfang (19.2°N, 108.8°E). By combining ray-path geometry with ionospheric characteristic parameters, the proposed technique generates MUF profiles through nonlinear fitting of ground distance and operating frequency. This method demonstrates better performance in low-latitude regions compared to the linear fitting approach traditionally used by HF radars at high latitudes. Validation against ionosonde data at Ledong (18.4°N, 109°E) shows close agreement in diurnal variation trends. The results reveal MUF asymmetries driven by the equatorial ionization anomaly (EIA) and local time effects—features that cannot be captured by empirical models or single ionosonde measurements. This approach addresses a critical gap in real-time MUF monitoring over oceanic regions, enhancing the reliability of HF communication links in maritime and emergency scenarios.
author: Lianhuan Hu, Guozhu Li, Baiqi Ning, Wenjie Sun, Guofeng Dai, Xiukuan Zhao, Haiyong Xie, Yi Li, Jianfei Liu

title: MAE-GAN: A Multifeature Adaptive Enhancement Approach for Multiangle Spaceborne InSAR Complex-Valued Images
content: Multiangle spaceborne interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) offers comprehensive observational capabilities by capturing additional angle-dependent information from multiple observation angles. It enables the retrieval of parameters that cannot be obtained from a single-angle observation, such as 3-D deformation. However, variations in observation geometries and platform configurations often lead to noticeable differences in image features, including spatial resolution, texture, and noise level, which present significant challenges for joint processing and consequently degrade the accuracy of parameter retrieval. To address these challenges, a multifeature adaptive enhancement method based on a generative adversarial network (MAE-GAN) is proposed. The network adaptively enhances the InSAR complex-valued image while simultaneously preserving additional angle-dependent information such as phase and scattering characteristics. Specifically, a cross-domain cycle (CDC) adversarial learning is employed to align images from low-quality angles with those from the highest-quality angle in the deep feature space, thereby enhancing the quality of low-quality observations. Furthermore, a fractal loss is introduced to protect additional angle-dependent information by maintaining self-similarity across scales. The experimental results on both simulated multiangle datasets and real multiangle space-surface bistatic synthetic aperture radar (SS-BSAR) measurements demonstrate that MAE-GAN achieves balanced improvements in texture consistency, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), spatial resolution, and phase accuracy. These results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in enhancing the quality and reliability of multiangle InSAR complex-valued images.
author: Yuanhao Li, Xingzhe Zhao, Zhiyang Chen, Xin Xie, Cheng Hu

title: The Influence of Practical Factors on the Cross Correlation and Brightness Temperature Image in 2-D MAS
content: The mirrored aperture synthesis (MAS) was proposed to improve the spatial resolution of passive microwave remote sensing. In MAS, the cross correlation is the radiometer output, directly influencing the brightness temperature (BT) image. In this article, the scene is divided into several regions based on whether the reflected signals can be received. Based on the scene division, the formulas for the cross correlation and the BT image are derived considering practical factors. The influence of practical factors on the scene division, cross correlation, and BT image is analyzed. These practical factors include the normalized antenna pattern, the reflector reflectivity, the reflector size, and the distance between the scene and the origin of the 2-D MAS. The normalized antenna pattern modulates the BT in various directions. The reflector reflectivity attenuates the intensity of the reflected signals. The reflector size determines the scene division. The distance between the scene and the origin influences the path difference between received signals. The influence of these practical factors on the scene division, cross correlation, and BT image is validated through numerical simulations and experiments. Based on these results, a near-field phase correction method is developed, and hints for mitigating the influence of practical factors are discussed.
author: Yuhang Huang, Qingxia Li, Zhaowen Wu, Zihuan Yu, Ke Chen, Rong Jin

title: Spatiotemporal Fusion Based on Cloudy Time-Series
content: Spatiotemporal fusion is a technique to blend images with fine spatial resolution (e.g., Landsat) and fine temporal resolution [e.g., Moderate-Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS)] to generate images with both fine spatial and temporal resolutions. This process typically requires a coarse spatial resolution image at the prediction time and at least one corresponding coarse-fine image pair at a known time. However, frequent cloud cover always limits the availability of spatially complete image pairs temporally close to the prediction time, leading to significant land cover changes that severely compromise fusion accuracy. To deal with this issue, a cloudy time-series-based spatiotemporal fusion (C-STF) method was proposed, which effectively utilizes valid information from cloud-contaminated time-series. Different from conventional approaches that depend on spatially complete auxiliary images, C-STF progressively reconstructs fine spatial resolution images by integrating cloudy observations in a stepwise manner, transforming the fusion task into an incremental gap-filling process. In addition, Sentinel-2 and 250 m MODIS images are incorporated to provide more temporal observations and enhance spatial details. Evaluations across four regions (with simulated clouds) and three Landsat tiles (with real clouds) demonstrate that C-STF achieves greater accuracy than both the unpaired spatiotemporal fusion of image patches (USTFIPs) and the cloudy time-series-based spatial and temporal adaptive reflectance fusion model (STARFM) (hereafter, simplified to STARFM) methods, with average correlation coefficient (CC) increases of 0.0768 and 0.0363 across all seven study areas, respectively. The combined use of Sentinel-2 and 250 m MODIS data further improves fusion performance. Furthermore, C-STF is suitable for time-series with various percentages of cloud cover.
author: Yijie Tang, Qunming Wang, Peter M. Atkinson

title: Adaptive Contourlet-Guided Fuzzy Fusion Network for Joint Classification of Hyperspectral and LiDAR Data
content: In recent years, the joint classification research of hyperspectral images (HSIs) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data has garnered significant attention in the field of Earth observation. The information complementarity between them has significantly improved the accuracy of image classification, and multisource remote sensing data fusion has become a current research hotspot. However, existing methods still face some challenges. On the one hand, the ability to capture the fine and smooth contour information is insufficient, which leads to the insufficient extraction of spatial structure features; on the other hand, the lack of modeling the uncertain relationship between modal features restricts the efficient complementarity of cross-modal information. To alleviate the above issues, this article proposes an adaptive contourlet-guided fuzzy fusion network (ACFFN) for joint classification of hyperspectral and LiDAR data. First, an adaptive spectral–spatial contourlet (ASSC) module is proposed, which utilizes LiDAR geometric priors to extract multiscale and multidirectional spatial features, and couples spectral attention to achieve collaborative fusion of spatial–spectral features. Then, a cross-modal feature fusion mechanism based on fuzzy learning is proposed, introducing fuzzy theory to model the uncertainty distribution of features to achieve adaptive cross-modal fusion. In addition, a morphology-guided attention (MGA) module is designed, which enhances elevation structural features through learnable morphological kernels and dynamic attention weights. Experiments and analysis on four public datasets show that our proposed method outperforms some of the most advanced methods in classification performance. The code link of the proposed method is https://github.com/Zzp-12/ACFFN
author: Zhipeng Zhong, Cuiping Shi, Weiwei Sun, Keyan Wang, Yuan Zhang

title: Hyperspectral Remote Sensing Object Detection via Cross-Domain Learning From Visible Images
content: Due to the high cost of data acquisition and annotation, it is challenging to obtain large-scale training data for hyperspectral remote sensing images (HRSIs), making it difficult to train object detectors directly. In contrast, visible-light remote sensing images (VLRSIs) are abundant and well-annotated. Hence, cross-domain object detection from VLRSI to HRSI (VLRSI2HRSI) provides a feasible solution for HRSI detection. However, existing cross-domain detection methods are developed for natural images. They neglect the shared spatial characteristics in the remote sensing scenario and fail to model the spectral properties of HRSIs. As a result, directly applying them to VLRSI2HRSI leads to significant performance degradation. To address this issue, we propose a visible-light-to-hyperspectral cross-domain detection network (VHCDN). We first observe that reconstructed residual representations in both VLRSI and HRSI suppress background and emphasize foreground objects. Based on this observation, we introduce a residual extracting module (REM) to extract domain-shared spatial features. Furthermore, since objects of the same semantic class may consist of different materials and exhibit spectral variations under varying illumination, we design a spectral saliency head (SSH) that models spectral uncertainty. By introducing one HRSI into the source-domain VLRSI datasets to guide the generation of spectral features, the SSH is trained with a spectral heteroscedastic loss and a spectral uncertainty loss, enabling the uncertainty learned in the labeled source domain to be stably transferred to the unlabeled target domain. Experiments on three cross-domain settings against seven representative methods demonstrate that VHCDN achieves state-of-the-art performance, while ablation studies verify the effectiveness of each proposed module.
author: Hongqi Zhang, He Sun, Hongmin Gao, Lianru Gao, Bing Zhang

title: Representative Spectral Correlation Network for Multisource Remote Sensing Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) and synthetic aperture radar (SAR)/light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data offer complementary spectral and structural information for land-cover classification. However, their effective fusion remains challenging due to two major limitations: the spectral redundancy in high-dimensional HSI and the heterogeneous characteristics between multisource data. To this end, we propose a representative spectral correlation network (RSCNet), a novel multisource image classification framework specifically designed to address the above challenges through spectral selection and adaptive interaction. The network incorporates two key components: 1) key band selection module (KBSM) that adaptively selects task-relevant spectral bands from the original HSI under cross-source guidance, thereby alleviating redundancy and mitigating information loss from conventional principal component analysis (PCA)-based spectral reduction. Moreover, the learned band subset exhibits highly discriminative spectral structures that align with discriminative semantic cues, promoting compact yet expressive representations, and 2) cross-source adaptive fusion module (CAFM) that performs cross-source attention weighting and local–global contextual refinement to enhance cross-source feature interaction. Experiments on three public benchmark datasets demonstrate that our RSCNet achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art methods, while maintaining substantially lower computational complexity. Our codes are publicly available at https://github.com/oucailab/RSCNet
author: Chuanzheng Gong, Feng Gao, Junyan Lin, Junyu Dong, Qian Du

title: A Detail Injection-Based Fusion Framework for Hyperspectral, Multispectral, and Panchromatic Remote Sensing Images
content: The primary goal of fusing hyperspectral image (HSI), multispectral image (MSI), and panchromatic (PAN) images is to transfer spatial details from MSI and PAN into HSI to generate high-resolution HSIs (HR-HSIs). Nevertheless, most existing methods rely on black-box deep learning (DL) architectures and rarely leverage this fundamental principle, resulting in limited physical interpretability. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel detail injection-based model for hyperspectral, multispectral, and panchromatic imagery (DIM-HMPF). The framework is engineered to enrich the spatial detail of HSI through fusion with MSI and PAN modalities, all while maintaining high-fidelity spectral information. Therefore, we utilize the physical model of detail injection for unfolding, using DL to learn this process. We further employ an optimization problem-solving method based on the proximal gradient descent (PGD) algorithm, achieved through a series of iterative steps. By unfolding these iterative steps into several network modules, DIM-HMPF is ultimately obtained. On the representative remote sensing hyperspectral datasets of Chikusei, Houston, and Xiongan, both quantitative evaluation metrics and visual comparison results consistently demonstrate that DIM-HMPF significantly outperforms existing mainstream fusion algorithms in terms of reconstruction accuracy, especially as evidenced by its superior performance in structural preservation and spectral fidelity. These results comprehensively validate the distinct superiority, robustness, and effectiveness of DIM-HMPF within the HMPF framework, as well as its strong generalization capability and practical potential in complex scenarios. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Freelancefangjian/DIM-HMPF
author: Jian Fang, He Sun, Xu Sun, Li Ni, Lianru Gao

title: MIFTr: Efficient Universal Image Matching With Modality-Invariant Feature Transformer
content: Image matching is a fundamental process in intelligent image processing, especially for the wide application of multisource remote sensing technologies, where invariant feature extraction is a core technology. Deep learning-based matching networks have emerged in recent years due to their powerful perception capabilities and parallel computing efficiency. However, most of them are designed for optical or a specific modality. Recently, general cross-modal matching has gained attention, but the prevailing approaches mainly rely on fine-tuning existing models or utilizing frozen weights from large pretrained models, with little focus on dedicated models tailored for cross-modal scenarios. In this article, we propose modality-invariant feature Transformer (MIFTr), a lightweight image-matching model dedicated to achieving universal cross-modal capability. The model innovatively introduces a rotational multibranch backbone, and incorporates the latest MambaVision mixer to construct a symmetric cross-vision-Mamba (CrossVM) module as the core interactive feature encoder. With sufficient pretraining, the model handles general types of image modalities with processing time within 40 ms per  $\textbf {512}^{\textbf {2}}$  sample on RTX4090. Through extensive comparative experiments on several complex cross-modal datasets, the proposed MIFTr demonstrates consistently superior performance on each modality, while maintaining a minimal model size and exceptional computational efficiency. The codes and experimental data will be made publicly available at https://github.com/MrPingQi/MIFTr
author: Chenzhong Gao, Yunhao Gao, Desheng Weng, Jixuan Li, Wei Li, Ran Tao, Xiang-Gen Xia

title: FAD-Net: Frequency-Domain Amplitude-Phase Decoupling Network for Optical-Elevation Remote Sensing Segmentation
content: Multimodal remote sensing semantic segmentation leverages multisource information for fine-grained scene analysis. However, due to the inherent limitations of spatial-domain representations, achieving effective feature interaction and reconstruction remains challenging. In this article, we propose the frequency-domain amplitude-phase decoupling network (FAD-Net). To resolve the fusion bottleneck caused by heterogeneous feature misalignment in the spatial domain, we design the multimodal Mona (MM-Mona) module. It establishes an interaction mechanism based on decoupled attributes recombination, physically decoupling textural and structural components to achieve complementarity between heterogeneous modalities. To address mutual interference between deep and shallow features in spatial decoding strategies, we propose the amplitude-phase recombination pyramid (APRP) decoder. A frequency-domain recombination mechanism is designed to reconstruct shallow details without losing deep semantic priors, guided by phase-geometric constraints. This effectively resolves the contradiction between preserving semantic consistency and recovering spatial details. Experimental results on the ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam datasets confirm that FAD-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. The source code will be made available at https://github.com/Apsaras2005/FAD-Net
author: Zicong Li, Xiaotong Li, Hao Zhu, Weibin Li, Biao Hou

title: Remote Sensing-Oriented Small-Object Detection Based on Rotation-Normalized Prompting Segment Anything Model
content: Remote sensing object detection is critical for Earth observation and analysis. However, accurately detecting small objects remains a significant challenge due to complex and heterogeneous backgrounds as well as the inherently limited information these objects provide. While numerous specialized algorithms have been proposed to enhance small-object detection, the weak feature representation of objects within cluttered scenes continues to severely limit performance, making this a persistent and open research problem. Therefore, in this article, to enhance the discriminative representation of small objects in a complex background, a novel two-phase rotation-normalized prompting-oriented small-object detection (RNP-OSOD) framework is proposed. In the first mask generation phase, a rotation-normalized prompt for segment anything model (RNP-SAM) is proposed to obtain high-quality instance and semantic mask annotations. In this method, image patches containing objects are cropped, enlarged, and rotation-normalized to acquire RNP before being fed into SAM, which then produces accurate mask supervision for training. In the second mask supervising object detection phase, to amplify the feature responses of small objects and balance instances across different scales, a scale-adaptive instance focusing (SAIF) loss is proposed by employing the generated masks as supervisory signals. This loss comprises an area-guided semantic reconstruction loss, called scale-adaptive semantic (SAS) loss and a detection-decoupled instance segmentation loss. To rigorously evaluate the proposed framework, we conduct extensive experiments on the small object detection datasets (SODAs) and create a new dataset, Small-DOTA, constructed by filtering and downsampling DOTA to match SODA’s characteristics. The experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across both benchmarks. The source code will be available at https://github.com/cbq233333/RNP-OSOD
author: Bingqian Chai, Jue Wang, Zhuo Zheng, Liang Chen, Xiaodong Gong, Wenchao Liu

title: Anchor-SAM: Active Mining of Latent Anchors From SAM Encoder for Road Extraction
content: Road extraction in high-resolution remote sensing imagery remains a persistent challenge due to occlusions and complex backgrounds, which lead to fragmented road topologies. However, existing road extraction methods constrained by limited pretraining remote sensing data often lack the generalization capability to distinguish roads from complex backgrounds. To address this issue, we propose Anchor-SAM, a novel framework that actively mines latent semantic anchors embedded in the SAM encoder to guide topological reconstruction. Our approach stems from a pivotal insight: the SAM encoder is able to abstract complex scenes into sparse semantic anchors at deep layers, thereby implicitly encoding the global structural skeleton. To harness these implicit cues, we introduce the multiscale deformable context perceiver (MDCP) and the deformable Bayesian conditional interaction module (DBCIM). The MDCP explicitly utilizes spatial cues to aggregate global semantics across distributed anchors, establishing a robust initial context for the decoder. The DBCIM facilitates the diffusion of semantic cues to surrounding regions and effectively suppresses noise. Specifically, by leveraging the semantic certainty of anchors to guide deformable sampling trajectories, this mechanism proactively filters out background regions while precisely repairing fragmented road topologies. Our method achieves competitive performance on both the DeepGlobe and Massachusetts datasets. The source code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Hmbb0606/Anchor-SAM
author: Wenhai Li, Xiaohui Huang, Xiaofei Yang, Yicong Zhou, Jiangtao Peng, Yifang Ban, Nan Jiang

title: Multilevel Feature Adaptation and Aggregation of Vision Foundation Model for Remote Sensing Scene Classification
content: Remote sensing scene classification (RSC) is crucial for image content interpretation and land function identification. Vision foundation models (VFMs) have strong general visual representation capabilities, which will enhance the feature extraction from remote sensing images and improve scene classification performances. However, there are obvious discrepancies in datasets and tasks between VFMs and RSC, which will impact the accuracy of scene classification. This article proposes a VFM-based framework with a multilevel feature adaptation and aggregation approach, labeled as VFM-FAA, for RSC, which can effectively exploit and utilize VFMs’ advantages for the downstream RSC task. Specifically, VFM-FAA adapts the VFM of DINOV2 for RSC from the perspectives of the dataset and task characteristics of the downstream task, respectively. First, considering that there are diverse objects with various sizes in remote sensing images, this article builds a multiscale adapter (MSA) based on the frozen VFM, thereby boosting feature extraction from remote sensing images. Second, this article proposes a multilevel feature aggregation (MFA) module to predict the scene category, which can comprehensively capture the discriminative semantic features of various scenes and further improve scene classification accuracy. Experimental results on three public datasets have demonstrated the efficiency and significant advantages of the proposed method on RSC. The proposed method can effectively enhance the generalization of the VFM in the RSC task and reduce the reliance on labeled training data, which has distinct advantages when there is a small amount of labeled training data. Compared with existing methods, VFM-FAA acquires accuracy performance gains by 2.78% in the AID dataset with 20% training samples, and improves the classification accuracy by 1.93% and 2.48% in the NWPU-RESISC45 dataset with 10% and 20% training samples, respectively.
author: Chonghua Lv, Dan Zhang, Dou Quan, Ning Huyan, Jiaqi Chen, Shuang Wang, Licheng Jiao

title: Few-Shot Target Recognition of Remote Sensing Images Based on Causal Consistency and Domain-Sensitive Channel Regularization
content: Remote sensing target recognition is significantly impacted by domain shifts between the source domain (SD) and target domain (TD), especially under few-shot conditions, where models struggle to extract effective features. Current methods emphasize extracting features that are invariant across domains, yet they often neglect the influence of domain-sensitive structures within convolutional networks, which hinders the overall generalization performance of the model. To address this issue, a novel few-shot domain generalization method named causal consistency and domain-sensitive channel regularization is proposed for remote sensing target recognition. Within the proposed framework, causal consistency learning is introduced to capture key causal features of target categories, mitigating the interference of noncausal features during inference. In addition, a domain-sensitive channel regularization module is designed to identify and suppress convolutional kernels with unstable activations caused by domain shifts, thereby preserving the structural robustness of the network and enhancing cross-domain feature extraction. To further stabilize training, a structural bias attenuation loss is incorporated to mitigate the instability introduced by kernel suppression, ensuring the model maintains robust generalization across different domain environments. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in suppressing the impact of domain-sensitive structures, improving domain-invariant feature extraction, and achieving superior recognition performance under domain shift conditions compared to existing approaches.
author: Jie Lai, Jie Geng, Weichen Ma, Wen Jiang

title: Topological Prior-Embedded Spatial–Temporal Tensor Approximation Model for Infrared Small-Target Detection
content: Infrared small-target detection (TD) is crucial in both military and civilian applications, yet it remains challenging to distinguish true targets from complex backgrounds and target-like flickering noise. To address this issue, a novel topological prior-embedded spatial–temporal tensor approximation (TP-STTA) model is proposed. The framework first designs a multitopological feature extraction (MTFE) model, in which area, diagonal, and volume attributes are explored, which effectively characterize the spatial properties of small targets and suppress background clutter and flickering noise. These features not only provide an auxiliary detection response but, more importantly, serve as target-aware priors to guide the subsequent tensor-based optimization. Building upon this, a multiprior tensor approximation (MPTA) model jointly exploits spatial–temporal correlations by integrating the multiattribute topological priors from MTFE with background-oriented smoothness and low-rank constraints, leading to stable and robust target enhancement. Finally, the responses from MTFE and MPTA branches are fused, and temporal trajectory consistency is exploited to further suppress false alarms. The experimental results on six real infrared sequences demonstrate that the proposed TP-STTA achieves accurate and reliable small TD with significant background suppression (BS).
author: Mingjing Zhao, Qi Ming, Yingjie Ma, Lu Li, Wei Li, Ran Tao

title: Diffusion Distillation for Remote Sensing Salient Object Detection
content: Salient object detection (SOD) in optical remote sensing images (ORSIs) is challenging due to limited spatial details, large-scale variation, and cluttered backgrounds with strong look-alike interference. Although diffusion models can progressively refine object structures and boundaries, their multistep sampling introduces high latency, which limits practical deployment over large areas. This article proposes semantic-guided diffusion distillation network (SGDDiff), which distills a high-capacity feature-space diffusion teacher into a compact student, thereby retaining diffusion-style refinement while enabling efficient inference. The student is trained under the supervision of a training-only pixel-semantic guidance (PSG) mechanism and intermediate decoder feature alignment. To strengthen representation with low computational overhead, we further develop a lightweight VD encoder (variational gated recurrent neural network (VGRNN)–DyT encoder), which integrates a VGRNN-based refinement unit with a DyT-enhanced Swin-style context block, where the VGRNN incorporates Vision Mamba-based spatial modeling and is further enhanced by a gated depthwise block (GDB). A two-stage training pipeline is adopted, including encoder–decoder pretraining and feature-aligned diffusion distillation. Extensive experiments on multiple ORSI-SOD benchmarks demonstrate that SGDDiff achieves state-of-the-art performance among 18 competing methods while substantially reducing computational overhead, yielding a favorable accuracy–efficiency tradeoff. The code and models are available at https://github.com/zizizirrr/SGDDIff
author: Ziran Gao, Huilan Luo

title: RSPoint-SAM: A SAM-Driven Method With Cross-Level Feature Fusion for Point Supervised Segmentation
content: Deep learning (DL) has made significant progress in the semantic segmentation of remote sensing (RS) images. However, its great performance heavily depends on high-quality annotations, which greatly restrict further advancement. Recently, the segment anything model (SAM), enabled by large-scale pretraining and flexible prompt mechanisms, has shown exceptional zero-shot segmentation ability for any object. Hence, we devise RSPoint-SAM, a SAM-based point-supervised semantic segmentation model that can achieve excellent results using point annotations. RSPoint-SAM has two main branches: convolutional neural network (CNN) branch and SAM-driven fusion branch. The CNN branch uses EfficientNet as its feature extraction backbone and employs a cross-level feature fusion strategy to combine multilevel features, effectively capturing local contextual dependencies under sparse supervision. Next, high-confidence pseudo-labels are produced by combining the first two outputs of the CNN branch with a credible assessment mechanism. The SAM-driven fusion branch then integrates EfficientViT and EfficientNet to utilize the ability of both global context modeling of the vision transformer (ViT) and the structured boundary perception of SAM, thereby capturing object-level semantics under limited pseudo-label supervision. Moreover, we develop a knowledge dynamic interaction mechanism to feed global semantic and boundary information from the SAM-driven fusion branch back into the CNN branch, reducing overfitting and further reinforcing pseudo-label quality. Experiments on two benchmark datasets show that RSPoint-SAM outperforms state-of-the-art weakly supervised (WS) methods. The source code will be available at: https://github.com/Yujia73/RSPoint-SAM.
author: Yujia Chen, Hao Cui, Zuohang Wu, Bin Lei, Guo Zhang, Li Zhang, Chunyang Zhu, Tian Feng, Gui Gao

title: Dual-Teacher Prototype Consistency for Unsupervised Domain Adaptive Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
content: Unsupervised domain-adaptive object detection (UDA-OD) in remote sensing images aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, mitigating performance degradation caused by domain shift. Existing methods commonly adopt a teacher–student framework, where a single teacher, updated via an exponential moving average (EMA) of the student parameters, generates pseudolabels for training. While this framework effectively transfers knowledge from the teacher to the student, the quality of pseudolabels and the alignment of source- and target-domain features remain key challenges for reducing domain shift. To address these challenges, a dual-teacher prototype consistency (DTPC) method is proposed. Specifically, DTPC employs two available object detection models as teachers to provide complementary guidance for the student. The larger teacher, equipped with a deeper backbone and kept frozen, extracts domain-invariant prototype anchors through a class-aware prototype update (CAPU) mechanism, thereby facilitating effective feature-level alignment between the source and target domains. Furthermore, the larger teacher and an EMA-updated smaller teacher jointly generate pseudolabels to supervise the student with soft- and hard-label losses, improving the quality and reliability of pseudolabels. Extensive experiments on two remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that DTPC achieves the state-of-the-art (SOTA) results, surpassing the second-best methods by 4.6% and 4.3% mean average precision (mAP) on the NWPU VHR- $10~\rightarrow $  detectIon in optical remote sensing images (DIOR) and VisDrone → DroneVehicle adaptation settings, respectively.
author: Bin Sun, Na Yan, Fuhua Zhang, Shutao Li

title: Enhancing Frequency Response in Implicit Full Waveform Inversion via Fourier Encoding
content: Implicit full waveform inversion (IFWI) has emerged as a promising framework for reconstructing complex subsurface structures. However, conventional methods suffer from a fundamental bias conflict, in which the inherent spectral bias of neural networks, such as the low-frequency bias of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and the high-frequency bias of sinusoidal representation networks (SIRENs), conflicts with the physics-based gradient’s requirement for progressive frequency reconstruction. To resolve this, we propose Encoding-IFWI, a decoupled spectral preconditioning framework. Unlike existing approaches that couple spectral bias with network architecture, our method utilizes Fourier encoding to engineer a neutral, broadband kernel basis. Theoretical analysis based on the neural tangent kernel (NTK) reveals that this design empowers the physics-based gradient to select requisite frequency components, allowing the wave equation residuals to autonomously guide the spectral evolution of the model. Comprehensive validation on Marmousi, Overthrust, and SEG/EAGE Salt models confirms that Encoding-IFWI significantly improves convergence speed and imaging resolution, offering a robust solution that effectively balances large-scale structural continuity with fine-scale geological details.
author: Luotao Zhang, Xiaobo Zhang, Shuo Han, Lingyi Cong, Zhengrong Wei

title: 3-D Transient Electromagnetic Inversion With Magnetic Induction Data: Incorporating Waveform Effects
content: Conventional transient electromagnetic (TEM) surveys primarily measure two types of responses: the time derivative of the magnetic induction ( $\mathrm {d} {\mathbf { b}}/\mathrm {d}t$ ) and the electric field ( $\bf e$ ). Inversion techniques for these data are well established. In contrast, the magnetic induction ( $\bf b$ ) response, measurable with low-noise total-field magnetometers (e.g., superconductive quantum interference devices (SQUIDs) or fluxgate sensors), remains underutilized in practice, and robust 3-D inversion methods for its quantitative interpretation are still lacking. Moreover, nonideal transmitter waveforms significantly impact all three TEM responses ( $\bf e$ ,  $\mathrm {d} {\mathbf { b}}/\mathrm {d}t$ , and  $\bf b$ ), and forward modeling and inversion must incorporate these effects properly. To address these issues, this study presents 3-D TEM forward modeling and inversion algorithms that compute responses directly in the time domain with a second-order backward Euler scheme. Waveform effects are accurately integrated via a convolution-based approach, which relaxes time-step constraints and enhances computational efficiency. The inverse problem is solved with the limited-memory Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (L-BFGS) optimization method. By leveraging the convolution approach, we formulate sensitivity computations for  $\bf e$ ,  $\mathrm {d} {\mathbf { b}}/\mathrm {d}t$ , and particularly  $\bf b$  responses, fully incorporating waveform effects. The algorithms are validated through numerical examples and field-data inversion. Sensitivity analyses reveal that  $\bf b$  data exhibit a broader spatial sensitivity range than  $\mathrm {d} {\mathbf { b}}/\mathrm {d}t$  data, suggesting a greater potential for imaging deeper geological targets. For both  $\mathrm {d} {\mathbf { b}}/\mathrm {d}t$  and  $\bf b$  data, sensitivity decreases markedly beneath the transmitter loop center, hindering target reconstruction in that region. This work advances the practical applicability of TEM inversion by enabling integrated handling of diverse TEM data types under realistic waveform conditions.
author: Jingyu Gao, Yan Dong, Jiankai Li, Miao Peng, Changhong Lin, Handong Tan, Thorkild Maack Rasmussen, Maxim Smirnov

title: Mitigating Predicted Misfit in Optimal Transport-Based Iterative Marchenko Multiple Elimination
content: Accurate reconstruction of subsurface structures represents a critical challenge in wave-equation inversion and resource exploration. The Born approximation, which assumes primary dominance, serves as the theoretical foundation for imaging and inversion methods. However, strong multiple reflections violate this assumption, leading to misinterpretation of mid-deep structures and distortion in reservoir assessment. Although the Marchenko multiple elimination (MME) method possesses theoretical advantages, its practical application is constrained by input data requirements such as ultrahigh signal-to-noise ratio and ideally source wavelet deconvolution. These limitations stem from MME’s purely numerical iteration process, which propagates errors and generates noncausal artifacts when the aforementioned requirements are not perfectly met. Conventional approaches merely adapt to MME’s requirements through data preprocessing without addressing the fundamental limitation of its iterative algorithm: the lack of a regional constraint. This study proposes an optimal transport-based Marchenko iterative framework that utilizes the Wasserstein distance as an objective, transforming the originally pointwise numerical correlation-based iteration into a regionally constrained distribution system. By minimizing the distributional discrepancy between predicted and observed wavefields, our method simultaneously resolves phase and amplitude mismatches, fundamentally suppressing error propagation. Validation using both synthetic and field data demonstrates that the proposed approach significantly enhances the stability, accuracy, and computational efficiency of multiple suppression, providing reliable support for mid-deep imaging and complex reservoir characterization.
author: Haoxin Zhu, Zhangqing Sun, Bin Hu, Jianwei Nie, Fei Jiang, Fuxing Han, Yang Zhang, Mingchen Liu, Zhenghui Gao

title: 3-D Modeling and Explicit Fréchet Derivative of the Crosswell Electromagnetic Measurement Using the Field-Based Secondary-Field Finite-Volume Method
content: In this article, we implement the field-based secondary-field 3-D finite-volume method (FVM) and the rapid direct method (RDM) of explicit Fréchet derivative for the crosswell electromagnetic (EM) measurement. The EM fields in the 3-D formation are decomposed into the background fields from the layered vertical transverse isotropy (VTI) formation and the secondary fields scattered by local anomalies. The 3-D scattered current density excited by each transmitter in local anomalies is discretized into numerous discrete current density elements. We employ the background field library and multivariate interpolation methods to enhance the efficiency of a large amount of calculation of the background fields for scattered current density. The discrete equations about the scattered electric field for all transmitters are solved simultaneously using the direct solver. The high-precision crosswell EM responses for multiple receivers at different depths are rapidly computed through interpolation and projection operators combined with the parallel technique. Based on the 3-D FVM and direct solver, we derive the explicit Fréchet derivative of the crosswell EM responses that involves calculations of only one forward simulation and simple back substitution. Through the same projection operator and parallel technique, we further enhance the computational efficiency of the explicit Fréchet derivatives. Finally, in the numerical results, the accuracy and efficiency of the forward and explicit Fréchet derivative are validated. The characteristics of the crosswell EM responses are systematically analyzed by deriving quantitative estimates of the explicit Fréchet derivative.
author: Lei Yu, Xiaoran Yu, Changchun Yin, Hongnian Wang, Xiuwen Mo, Yuhang Guo, Xiuyan Ren, Yang Su, Yunhe Liu, Bo Zhang

title: A Multiple Inclination Orbit Constellation With High-Low Satellite-to-Satellite Tracking for Weekly Gravity Field Recovery
content: Due to error sources arising from undersampling of target signals and imperfections in background models, the single-pair satellite gravity missions equipped with the low-low satellite-to-satellite tracking technology are of limited performance, such as the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment Follow-on (GFO). Adding a lower-inclination GFO-like mission to form the Bender constellation is a promising solution, but it entails high costs and long development cycles. To address this limitation, we propose a cost-effective solution, the multiple inclination orbit (MIO) constellation, which is equipped with mature high-low satellite-to-satellite tracking technology. The MIO constellation consists of 80 satellites across four orbital inclination groups (70°, 75°, 80°, 85°). The performance of these constellations in recovering 7-day time-variable gravity fields was evaluated through numerical simulations, using the root-mean-square error (RMSE) and the Nash–Sutcliffe efficiency coefficient as metrics for regional application. Spectral-domain results show that the GFO+MIO constellation improves spatial resolution to ∼666 km, approaching the ∼500 km resolution of the Bender solution. Spatial-domain results further demonstrate that the GFO+MIO solutions achieve performance comparable to the Bender constellation in: 1) hydrology applications in terms of equivalent water height [RMSE/NSE: 67.89 mm/0.64 (GFO+MIO) versus 59.28 mm/0.73 (Bender)] smoothed by a 300 km Gaussian filter; 2) gravity anomaly arising from the 2004 Sumatra Mw9.2 earthquake [RMSE/NSE: 4.16 $\mu Gal$/0.92 (GFO+MIO) versus 5.23 $\mu Gal$/0.87 (Bender)] smoothed by a 100 km Gaussian filter. The study provides critical insights for future mission designs aimed at advancing time-variable gravity field recovery for geophysics applications.
author: Guangyu Jian, Changqing Wang, Zitong Zhu, Bin Li, Min Zhong, Wei Feng

title: Exploring the Potential of Oil Concentration Estimation From Single-Polarization SAR Data
content: Quantitative estimation of oil concentration in marine oil spills is critical for effective emergency response. Previous studies on oil concentration retrieval have primarily relied on multipolarization Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery. In this work, we explore the potential of using single-polarization SAR imagery to estimate oil concentration. Oil spills appear as low backscatter areas in SAR images due to two mechanisms: i) damping of short Bragg waves, and ii) reduction of effective permittivity when the oil spill is sufficiently thick. To explore the feasibility of single-polarization SAR for oil concentration estimation, we assume that the damping effect on short waves is independent of oil concentration. Accordingly, the normalized radar cross section (NRCS) attenuation caused by wave damping can be separated from the total attenuation induced by the oil spill. The remaining NRCS reduction is then attributed to variations in effective permittivity associated with oil concentration. Based on this principle, oil concentration can be estimated from a single-polarization SAR image. The proposed method was applied to the Deepwater Horizon oil spill, and the retrieved concentrations showed good agreement with results from dual-polarized SAR when the oil concentration is sufficiently high (e.g., greater than 0.6 in our experiments). This study demonstrates that single-polarization SAR is applicable not only for oil spill detection but also for rapid estimation of oil concentration in thick, high-concentration marine oil spills.
author: Honglei Zheng, Yunhua Wang, Peng Ren, Weimin Huang

title: DS2AF: An Iterative Detection to Spectral Similarity-Guided Augmentation Framework for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) has greatly benefited from deep learning; however, the high dimensionality of hyperspectral data and the costly acquisition of labeled samples result in persistent sample scarcity, especially in real-world applications. Few-shot learning (FSL) addresses this challenge by enabling classification with only a few labeled samples per class, yet existing methods often struggle with HSIs due to complex spectral-spatial characteristics and substantial intraclass variability. This article proposes detection-to-spectral similarity-guided augmentation framework, an iterative FSL framework that incorporates a novel hyperspectral detection-to-similarity ratio (HDSR) algorithm. HDSR unifies three complementary perspectives—matched-filter, angular, and probabilistic—into a single ratio-based formulation, yielding a robust global similarity measure for reliable sample selection. Leveraging HDSR, a two-stage augmentation strategy is employed: an initial the regional augmentation leverages spatial locality to stabilize the initial model, preventing it from diverging when sample scarcity is extreme. This process establishes a strong foundation by enriching the local neighborhood of labeled samples. Once the model has developed preliminary discriminative capabilities, global augmentation then searches for more representative samples across the entire image domain, gradually expanding the training set without additional manual labeling. A multibranch classification network is further developed, comprising a full-band 1-D spectral branch, an attention-enhanced 2-D spatial branch, and a hybrid 3-D–2-D spectral-spatial branch to extract complementary features. Moreover, an adaptive stopping criterion based on classification consistency is introduced to ensure stability and prevent overfitting. Experiments on the Indian Pines, University of Pavia, and Salinas datasets under the 5-shot setting achieve overall accuracies of 83.47%, 93.01%, and 96.16%, respectively, substantially outperforming recent FSL approaches and confirming the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework.
author: Shih-Yu Chen, Yu-Cheng Wu, Kai-Quan Wu

title: Fusing ICESat-2 and MODIS Data to Estimate the Three-Dimensional Distribution of Marine Chlorophyll-a Concentration
content: Monitoring the three-dimensional (3-D) distribution of marine chlorophyll-a (Chla) concentration is essential to accurately assess the primary productivity levels and health status of marine ecosystems. However, traditional shipboard and buoy observations struggle to achieve large-scale synchronous monitoring, passive remote sensing cannot resolve vertical structure, and active lidar suffers from limited spatial coverage. To address these limitations, this study proposes an active–passive fusion-based estimation method that combines ICESat-2 vertical profile data with moderate resolution imaging spectroradiometers (MODIS) ocean color data to develop the Chla 3-D distribution estimation model through machine learning approaches. Four Bayesian-optimized ensemble learning models based on decision trees were trained and evaluated in the oligotrophic waters of the western North Atlantic. Among these, the RandomForest model performed the best, with the mean prediction accuracy measured by the determination coefficient (R2) of 0.86, the root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.0042 mg/m3, and the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 9.91%. Then, the Chla distributions across nine depth layers (2–18 m) were estimated through the RandomForest model, with MODIS data as input. Validated using spatiotemporally independent ICESat-2 retrievals and biogeochemical Argo float (BGC-Argo) data, the model demonstrates strong generalization capability and application potential, with the overall estimation accuracy reaching RMSE of 0.0053 mg/m3 and MAPE of 22.57%. This study overcomes the respective limitations of single-sensor approaches and provides a feasible technical solution for 3-D remote sensing monitoring of marine ecological environments.
author: Zepeng Wang, Qianqian Wang, Fangfang Zhang, Jinbo An, Shenglei Wang, Junsheng Li

title: Simulation of Active and Passive Satellite Observations and Estimation of Vegetation Optical Depth Across Different Frequencies Based on a Theoretical Scattering Model in the Tibetan Grassland
content: Multifrequency active and passive microwave remote sensing can deliver complementary insights for surface observations. In this study, we aim to simultaneously reproduce these observations and analyze their covariations, as well as to explore the impacts of frequency and observation mode on vegetation optical depth (VOD) estimation using the Tor Vergata (TVG) model. First, model calibration is implemented to simultaneously reproduce passive L- and C-band and active C-band observations, as well as their covariations in the Tibetan grassland. The calibrated TVG model is then employed for calculating multifrequency VOD values, intercomparing satellite retrievals across different frequencies and observations modes, and exploring active and passive covariations and the consistency of active and passive VODs across frequencies. Results demonstrate that the calibrated TVG model effectively reproduces the aforementioned passive and active observations, as well as the negative linear relationship identified in active and passive covariations. Most current satellite-based VOD products have difficulty in simultaneously capturing both frequency- and polarization dependent signals. In addition, the existing active VOD product exhibits an inconsistent seasonal variation compared with our estimates derived from the TVG model. Numerical simulations from the optimized model demonstrate the identified negative linear relationship consistently holds for L/L- and S/S-band active and passive covariations but may break down for the C/C-band combination. It is further confirmed that active and passive VODs exhibit consistency at the same frequency. These findings provide novel insights into integrating multifrequency microwave remote sensing observations to interpret land physical properties and estimating frequency-dependent VOD.
author: Xiaojing Bai, Donghai Zheng, Xiangzhuo Liu, Jie He

title: An Enhanced Hierarchical Framework for Mapping Urban Building Age Using Long-Term Landsat Time-Series Imagery
content: Accurate building age mapping is crucial for urban renewal, energy consumption estimation, and risk assessment. Recent studies used medium-resolution satellite image time series to estimate building ages by identifying breakpoints in spectral/index time series, making great advancement beyond the traditional single-year building façade analysis. However, their accuracy remains limited, which is primarily caused by the temporal mismatch between spectral/index time-series breakpoints and actual building completion years, and the conventional binary scheme (renewal versus nonrenewal), which cannot separate unchanged old buildings from sprawl areas. To address these challenges, we propose a three-layer hierarchical temporal recognition framework. First, a new set of long-term spectral/index change features, combined with a supervised classifier, is used to acquire more detailed building change categories: unchanged, sprawl, and renewal. Second, high-confidence construction time windows for sprawl and renewal buildings are identified by integrating empirical index-based breakpoint detection with a multiyear tolerance strategy. Third, building completion years within these windows are further refined using short-term spectral/index variation features and a data-driven modeling approach. The experimental results in Guangzhou, a city characterized by complex building age structures, demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: the new method outperforms the traditional breakpoint-based method by 16% in single-year accuracy and attains 87.16% accuracy under a two-year tolerance, matching the latter’s performance only at a three-year tolerance. The proposed framework produces a highly accurate and spatially detailed building age map, offering both a technical advancement in remote sensing urban analysis and a reliable data foundation for urban renewal planning and sustainable management.
author: Na Jie, Li Zhuo, Qiuping Li, Haiyan Tao

title: SC-DRGAN: A Cross-Site Transfer Learning Architecture for Tropical Forest Structural Parameters Estimation
content: Multibaseline polarimetric interferometric synthetic aperture radar (PolInSAR) has demonstrated considerable potential for estimating tropical forest canopy height and aboveground biomass (AGB). Conventional approaches often rely on spectral analysis or compressive sensing to retrieve vertical structure profiles, which are computationally intensive and highly dependent on forest structural properties, limiting their generalization across diverse geographical regions. To address these challenges, we introduce SC-DRGAN, a cross-site transfer learning framework that synergistically integrates multibaseline PolInSAR observations with Global-Ecosystem-Dynamics-Investigation-derived measurements to improve the quantification of canopy structural attributes. The proposed architecture incorporates three core components: a similarity calculation module, a deep ResNet-based feature extractor, and a generative adversarial network, collectively enhancing feature discriminability and cross-domain generalization. The model was evaluated over two tropical forest sites in Gabon, Lopé and Modah. The proposed SC-DRGAN model was rigorously benchmarked against six state-of-the-art methods, including KNN, random forest, neural networks (NNs), transformer, ResNet, and dual-channel transformer. The results indicate that SC-DRGAN achieves cross-site transfer $R^{2}$ of 0.76 for canopy height and 0.77 for AGB, outperforming deep learning benchmarks by 10%–64% in accuracy. The corresponding root mean square error reductions reached 2.11 m relative to a nine-layer NNs and 59.78 Mg/ha compared to the transformer model, underscoring the robustness and transferability of the proposed framework.
author: Wenmei Li, Liwen Chen, Xuanhao Liu, Jiadong Zhang, Yan Jia, Lei Zhao, Yuhong He

title: Multidimensional Comparative Study of Ionospheric Disturbance Responses During Storm Events in March and April 2023 in China
content: This article utilizes the China crustal movement observation network GNSS observations, Swarm/COSMIC satellite electron density measurements, and TIMED/GUVI thermospheric composition data to systematically analyze the multiscale ionospheric responses over China during two geomagnetic storm events in March 2023 (CME-driven) and April 2023 (CME-CIR compound driven). Key findings include the following. First, the March CME event exhibited a single-phase development pattern, with SYM-H dropping to −170 nT. The traveling ionospheric disturbances primarily propagate northwestward, synchronous with the dawnside gravity wave-dominated O/N2; front (>0.6) and the westward migration of detrended DTEC positive disturbances.
